bins.name: Preserve state retail jurisdiction
bins.desc: Talen argues that interconnecting a large load is inseparable from retail distribution, which the FPA reserves to the states, so FERC should not assert jurisdiction over large load interconnections. It warns the ANOPR leaves 'directly connected' undefined and that asserting jurisdiction would trigger lengthy federal/state litigation.
quotes: Talen Energy Corporation (“Talen”) files these reply comments to encourage the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“Commission” or “FERC”) to proceed with caution in any action taken following the proposed Advance Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“ANOPR”) in order to preserve the federal/state jurisdictional balance established under the Federal Power Act (“FPA”) and decades of precedent.
For end-use customers receiving retail service, the delivery of energy—particularly for large loads—is inseparable from retail distribution, which falls squarely within state jurisdiction.

the ANOPR fails to define the term “directly” and it remains unclear how a large load or hybrid facility could directly connect to the transmission system without also connecting with facilities that qualify as distribution facilities under the Commission’s seven-factor test.

attempting to resolve the threshold jurisdictional question posed in the ANOPR—namely, whether FERC should assert jurisdiction over large load interconnections—would undoubtedly trigger lengthy litigation between the Commission and the states.

bins.name: Hybrid facility connection ambiguity
bins.desc: Talen notes the ANOPR does not explain how a large load or hybrid facility could directly connect to transmission without also using facilities that qualify as distribution under the Commission's seven-factor test.
quotes: the ANOPR fails to define the term “directly” and it remains unclear how a large load or hybrid facility could directly connect to the transmission system without also connecting with facilities that qualify as distribution facilities under the Commission’s seven-factor test.

bins.name: Act through pending proceedings
bins.desc: Talen favors faster action through existing dockets with developed records rather than a slow new rulemaking, and asks FERC to direct PJM to expedite interconnection of resources selected under the backstop auction.
quotes: FERC can issue orders in any of the pending proceedings that already have fully developed records on a much faster timeline.
the Commission should direct PJM to develop a Reliability Resource Initiative-like interconnection process which will expedite the interconnection of all resources selected under the RBA mechanism.

bins.name: No discrimination against existing generation
bins.desc: Talen wants existing generation protected from undue discrimination, opposing BYOG mandates and disproportionate reliability studies that disadvantage loads contracting with existing units. It argues new and existing resources should compete on equal footing to avoid accelerating premature retirements.
quotes: Proposals that require new large load customers to bring their own new generation are discriminatory.

enhanced reliability studies that apply disproportionate requirements to large loads contracting with existing generation as opposed to new generation are unlawful and will result in undue discrimination against existing generation.
the Commission should allow existing generation resources to compete on equal footing with new generation resources to serve large load customers.
policies favoring new generation at the expense of existing resources risks accelerating retirement of highly reliable, dispatchable, and baseload units.

Conditioning customer interconnections on BYOG or instructing PJM to deny service conflicts with bedrock open-access principles FERC established in Order No. 888 and Order No. 2000, which mandate non-discriminatory transmission service under a Commission-approved tariff.

Instructing PJM to pick winners and losers among loads would undermine the Commission’s open-access framework and invite litigation over undue discrimination, delaying urgently needed reliability solutions.

bins.name: Studies must not discriminate
bins.desc: Talen supports cautious, balanced study requirements but opposes enhanced reliability studies that impose disproportionate burdens on large loads contracting with existing generation, calling such asymmetry unlawful discrimination.
quotes: Talen Energy Corporation (“Talen”) files these reply comments to encourage the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“Commission” or “FERC”) to proceed with caution in any action taken following the proposed Advance Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“ANOPR”) in order to preserve the federal/state jurisdictional balance established under the Federal Power Act (“FPA”) and decades of precedent.

enhanced reliability studies that apply disproportionate requirements to large loads contracting with existing generation as opposed to new generation are unlawful and will result in undue discrimination against existing generation.

bins.name: Reject BYOG and load denial
bins.desc: Talen opposes the PJM Market Monitor's bring-your-own-generation proposal and any authority for RTOs to deny or condition load interconnection on self-supplied generation, arguing it conflicts with open-access principles and creates a two-tiered load system.
quotes: Proposals that require new large load customers to bring their own new generation are discriminatory.

Conditioning customer interconnections on BYOG or instructing PJM to deny service conflicts with bedrock open-access principles FERC established in Order No. 888 and Order No. 2000, which mandate non-discriminatory transmission service under a Commission-approved tariff.
FERC should reject any proposal that allows PJM (or any other RTO/ISO) to deny interconnection or prioritize BYOG arrangements.

bins.name: PJM open access and backstop auction
bins.desc: Talen opposes giving PJM discretion to pick which loads interconnect or to prioritize BYOG, but supports modifying PJM's existing Reliability Backstop Auction and interconnection process as a market-based fix for resource adequacy.
quotes: Conditioning customer interconnections on BYOG or instructing PJM to deny service conflicts with bedrock open-access principles FERC established in Order No. 888 and Order No. 2000, which mandate non-discriminatory transmission service under a Commission-approved tariff.

Instructing PJM to pick winners and losers among loads would undermine the Commission’s open-access framework and invite litigation over undue discrimination, delaying urgently needed reliability solutions.
FERC should reject any proposal that allows PJM (or any other RTO/ISO) to deny interconnection or prioritize BYOG arrangements.

Talen suggests modifying the RBA so that the auction is triggered when the RPM auctions clear below 98% of the reserve margin requirement two base residual auctions in a row, rather than the current trigger of the base residual auctions clearing below 99% of the reserve margin three auctions in a row.
the Commission should direct PJM to develop a Reliability Resource Initiative-like interconnection process which will expedite the interconnection of all resources selected under the RBA mechanism.

bins.name: Resource adequacy is the core issue
bins.desc: Talen frames sufficient generation to meet forecasted demand growth as the underlying problem that large load interconnection rules alone will not solve.
quotes: The way to ensure all loads are reliably served is by ensuring there are sufficient generation resources to meet the forecasted exponential growth in demand.

bins.name: Market-based generation incentives
bins.desc: Talen argues accurate scarcity-reflecting price signals drive generation investment and proposes a sooner-triggering PJM Reliability Backstop Auction to stimulate new and retained generation through market mechanisms.
quotes: the Commission must ensure that price signals accurately reflect scarcity and reliability needs.  Only when markets send clear signals can developers make informed investment backed decisions.

Talen suggests modifying the RBA so that the auction is triggered when the RPM auctions clear below 98% of the reserve margin requirement two base residual auctions in a row, rather than the current trigger of the base residual auctions clearing below 99% of the reserve margin three auctions in a row.
